rankings.writeup: Sean Lee went nuclear against Nathan — 31 points on 13-for-22 shooting with a season-high 9 assists, arguably the best individual performance of the season. Christian Kim continued his absurd campaign with 27 points and 16 rebounds (7 offensive), drilling 5 threes along the way. Despite playing with just 5 players (Jon Han filled in), they held Nathan to a 70-70 draw against a full squad. At 3-1-1, Sean owns the league's best win percentage (.700) and the most terrifying two-man punch in the OBA. The only concern: Josh Paik went 1-for-9, and the depth beyond Sean and Christian remains thin. But when those two are cooking, it simply doesn't matter.

The 70-70 draw against Sean was an encouraging result after two straight losses — Nathan had the full squad together and it showed. Nathan Paik was dominant as usual with 23 points, 14 rebounds, and 5 assists, while Austin Im bounced back in a big way with 19 points on efficient 8-for-14 shooting (3-for-5 from three). The supporting cast stepped up: Ryan Choi nailed 3 threes for 13 points and Andrew Paik added 9/5/2 on 3-for-4 shooting. At 3-2-1 with the most games played (6) of any team, the overall talent remains among the league's best. The question is whether they can convert these close games into wins — three of their last four have been decided by 3 points or less (or a draw).

A tale of two games for Team Matt. On Saturday, Matt Paik delivered one of the most dominant performances in OBA history — 44 points on 15-for-24 shooting with 11 free throws to lead a 69-60 rout of Aron. Then a day later, the team fell 59-62 to PJo in a stunning upset where the supporting cast went cold. The Matt Paik dependency is becoming impossible to ignore: he's averaging 40%+ usage and when teams neutralize him (or he drops to 'just' 24), the cupboard is bare. Ben Ngo showed flashes across both games (14 and 12 points), but the heavy reliance on one player makes this team volatile. At 3-2, they're still above .500, but the loss to PJo is a warning sign.

Back-to-back losses have Team Aron sliding down the standings after a promising 2-1 start. The 69-60 loss to Matt was rough — Matt Paik hung 44 points on them and there was simply no answer. Aron Kim (18/7) and Jonathan Chung (17/4 with a block) did their part, but the supporting cast couldn't keep up. Brandon Kim chipped in 11, but Ryan Yum went 3-for-9 from the field (all three-point attempts) and David Son was a zero across the board. With Jon Han playing as a fill-in on other teams, the roster is thinner than it was early in the season. At 2-3, Aron Kim — who went undefeated in Season 1 — needs to will this group back on track before it's too late.

THE DROUGHT IS OVER. Team PJo finally broke through with a 62-59 upset over Matt, and it wasn't a fluke — they were the better team from start to finish. PJo was magnificent: 15 points, 13 rebounds, 5 assists, and 2 steals, dominating the glass against a bigger Matt frontline. Justin Ho led the scoring with 17, and four different players reached double figures (Isaac Seong 10, PJun 10). The balanced attack and the defense — holding Matt Paik to 24 on 9-for-19 — showed what this team is capable of when the shots fall. At 1-4, the record is still ugly, but the performance was anything but. Can they carry this momentum forward, or was it lightning in a bottle?
